content:
A Guide to Gavi

(Gah·vee)

Gavi DOCG is an Italian white wine region with impressive range and a strong sense of place. In this hilly corner of southeastern Piedmont, the Cortese grape finds its defining expression in vineyards shaped by altitude, exposure, and three distinct soil zones. The denomination’s moderate continental climate, marked by cold winters, hot summers, and breezes that blow inland from the Ligurian coast, helps give Gavi its hallmark freshness, finesse, and complexity, whether the wines are enjoyed young and floral or with more age and mineral depth.

Spread across roughly 1,600 hectares and 11 municipalities in the province of Alessandria, Gavi is both a wine appellation and destination. It’s a region with deep roots, with evidence of grape cultivation dating back to the Middle Ages, finally earning DOCG status in 1998. Today, that long-established wine culture is matched by a modern focus on quality and stewardship, from detailed vineyard mapping by altitude, exposure, and sunlight, to sustainability efforts that include environmental bio-monitoring with bees. And these impressive factors are only some of the reasons why Gavi remains one of Italy’s benchmark white wines.

By the Numbers

Vineyard Area: 1,600 hectares

Municipalities: 11 – Bosio, Carrosio, Capriata d’Orba, Francavilla Bisio, Gavi, Novi Ligure, Parodi Ligure, Pasturana, San Cristoforo, Serravalle Scrivia, and Tassarolo

DOCG Certification: 1998

Grape: 100% Cortese

Annual Production: About 14 million bottles

Distribution: Export to 100 countries

Grape Variety

Gavi wine is made from 100% Cortese, an indigenous Piedmontese white grape that has deep roots in this territory, tended by farmers from more than 250 local families. It is a vigorous varietal that requires careful pruning, minimal tillage, and selective thinning to ensure low yields and preserve quality.  

In the glass, Cortese shows bright citrus, white flowers, fresh fruit, and a signature bitter almond note. But the grape’s biggest selling point is its versatility. It can be zippy and aperitivo-ready, balanced and food friendly, or rich and complex with time in the bottle.

If Barolo and Barbaresco are Piedmont’s marquee reds, Gavi provides the perfect foil as the signature white. It has heritage, identity, and range, as well as a contemporary appeal that’s timeless in the glass and at the table.

History

The history of Gavi wine and its denomination is rooted in a long-standing viticultural tradition that dates back over a thousand years. 

Positioned along the ancient frontier between the sea and the plains, Gavi has been a strategic political and commercial foothold in the region for millennia. Dating back to the second century BC, the Roman city of Libarna was already an important trading hub. Located in present-day Serravalle Scrivia, it sat at the crossroads of three key Roman trade routes: the Via Postumia, Via Francigena, and the Salt Road. 

Nearly 1,500 years later, starting in the Middle Ages, the area developed under Genoese influence. The landscape was shaped by feudal estates, vineyards, forests, waterways, and early production sites such as mills, ironworks, and sawmills.

The earliest documented evidence of vine cultivation in Gavi can be traced to 972, under the authority of the Archbishop of Genoa.

By the late 18th century, Gavi wines had begun to gain recognition beyond local borders, with documented exports departing from the port of Genoa to the New World. 

The first dedicated plantings of the Corteste grape were established in 1856, followed by formal ampelographic studies that highlighted its suitability for high-quality wine production. By the early 20th century, Gavi became synonymous with the Cortese grape—essentially defining the region’s signature style of white wines.

In more recent decades, efforts have focused on quality and preservation. In 1998, Gavi was officially awarded DOCG status—the highest designation in the Italian wine system—defining its production area across the 11 municipalities.

Local Lore

Legend has it, the name Gavi comes from Princess Gavia, the fictional daughter of the real-life Frankish King Clodomer who made the region her home back in the 6th century. There are different versions of how and why Gavia ended up in the rolling hills of the area that would eventually bear her name. Some accounts claim she sought refuge from her enemies. Others say it was a case of forbidden love. She fell for a soldier her father didn’t approve of, so they ran away to a remote village in northwestern Italy.

No matter the case (or historical accuracy), this saga became intertwined with the identity of the territory, symbolising resilience, protection, and a deep connection between people and place. And the romantic myth continues to echo through the vineyards and landscapes of Gavi to this day.

This Region Is Tasted
by Jeff Porter

Jeff Porter reviews wines from Northern Italy (Piedmont, Lombardy, Liguria, Veneto, Alto Adige, Trentino, Friuli-Venezia Giulia), Central Italy (Emilia-Romagna, Marche & Umbria), Slovenia, and Switzerland. A renowned sommelier, Porter’s career began in Napa Valley, before joining acclaimed Osteria Mozza in Los Angeles, followed by Del Posto Ristorante in NYC, and eventually taking the helm as beverage operations director for Bastianich Hospitality Group. Today, he creates beverage lists for a roster of clients around the world and reviews thousands of bottles a year for Wine Enthusiast.

Soils and Styles

Gavi is generally perceived as fresh, easy-drinking white wine, appreciated for its immediacy and vibrancy. Yet, with time in bottle, Gavi sheds its youthful simplicity for complexity and depth. Tertiary notes of honey, dried fruit, and more pronounced mineral nuances emerge as the structure broadens, acidity integrates, and wine evolves into a rich, ageworthy wine that deserves a spot in the cellar.

Northern Belt
In the northern part of Gavi, red clay soils rich in iron from alluvial deposits help produce wines with more body and structure. This is the warmest part of the denomination, and the wines here often show a little more breadth and weight.

Central Strip
This belt is made up of alternating marl and sandstone, along with clays, sands, and pebbles. This mix yields wines with a compelling balance of flavor, texture, and distinct freshness.

Southern Belt
In the southernmost part of Gavi where the vineyards increase in elevation and the Apennines draw closer, pale marine marls result in wines defined by more delicate, fine-boned expressions with marked lift and minerality.

A Study in Terroir

Gavi is by no means a one-note wine category. It is not defined by styles, but by soils. Here, the Cortese grape becomes a lens through which the land speaks—subtle, precise, and deeply expressive.

White soils, pale marls of ancient marine origin, are characterized by alternating layers of marl and sandstone. A mix of clays, sands, and pebbles can be found in fluvial terraces, marine formations, and rocks from the oceanic crust. This is where Gavi wines with great balance between structure and flavor come from.

Move to the red soils, richer in clay and iron, and the expression shifts. The wines gain breadth and warmth, offering riper fruit, a more generous mouthfeel, and a deeper structure. This is the area with the warmest climate, yielding Gavi wines with excellent body and structure.

Gavi is more than a fresh white wine: every bottle reveals a denomination rooted in nuance, where identity is shaped underground long before it reaches the glass.

A Versatile Partner for Pairing

Its most distinctive pairing is with the Ligurian-Piedmontese cuisine of this area: it works beautifully with ravioli, trofie al pesto, and classic Piedmontese dishes, from vitello tonnato to tajarin with truffle.

Yet Gavi’s true strength lies in its versatility. Beyond traditional Italian cuisine—seafood, vegetables, pasta, and fried dishes—it pairs effortlessly with a wide range of global flavors. From the precision and umami-driven profiles of Japanese cuisine to the aromatic spices of Middle Eastern dishes, to the bold contrasts of American fusion and the vibrant, fermented notes of Korean cooking, Gavi adapts with ease.

Its precision is key: it cleanses the palate, complements the dish, and never overpowers. In more structured and evolved expressions, it can also support more complex and layered preparations.

Not Just a Wine, But a Destination

More than just a wine, Gavi is a destination. Rolling hills shift in color and composition, revealing a landscape shaped by centuries of geologic and human history. Located just one hour and a half from Milan and Turin, and within easy reach of Genoa and the Ligurian coast, Gavi is a natural crossroads of culture and landscape. It is also just about an hour from Portofino and close to the renowned Serravalle Designer Outlet, making it an ideal destination that blends wine, leisure, and lifestyle.

For lovers of arts and culture, the Forte di Gavi and the archaeological site of Libarna are essential stops. Libarna stands as one of the most significant Roman sites in Northern Italy: an ancient city where traces of theaters, roads, and residential areas still tell the story of a vibrant commercial hub deeply connected to agriculture and wine production. Walking through its remains offers a tangible link to a past where Gavi was already a land of exchange, culture, and viticulture.

Sports and nature lovers can enjoy excursions on foot, horseback, or mountain bikes along trails that venture into the Apennine. From the top of these rollercoaster-like paths, the peak of Monte Tobbio (1092 m) offers stunning panoramas of the Ligurian Sea and the Gulf of Genoa. 

Here, every hill has its own distinct voice—and so does every glass.

title: These Zippy Sauvignon Blancs Are Crushing It
summary:
Zippy bottles from New Zealand, floral Sancerres, and more made our list of highly drinkable Sauvignon Blancs.

The post These Zippy Sauvignon Blancs Are Crushing It appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Bright, crisp, and zesty, there are few wines more refreshing than Sauvignon Blanc. (Though these Chardonnays are looking pretty delicious right now, too!) Hailing from France, this white grape is now planted all over the world, producing popular wines from its homeland as well as New Zealand, Italy, the United States, and beyond.

As Sauvignon Blanc’s location changes, so does its flavor profile. While French versions can shine with floral, grassy notes, Sauvy Bs from other parts of the world can veer more fruit-forward and even tropical.

Though it wasn’t easy, we narrowed down our list of the best bottles to 15—each one showcasing the grape’s diverse skillset.

New Zealand

Few places have made a grape variety their calling card the way New Zealand—and especially the region of Marlborough—have with Sauvignon Blanc.

Christina Pickard, who reviews New Zealand bottlings for Wine Enthusiast, puts it plainly. “Marlborough Sauvignon Blancs are never short on fruit,” she says. “In fact, that pop of citrus, tropical, and passionfruit—along with green herbal and summer vegetable garden characters—is what makes this wine style so recognizable and so wildly popular across the globe.”

But there’s far more variety here than the reputation for zingy freshness might suggest. More and more producers are opting to ferment their Sauvignon Blanc in oak barrels (a distinctly French strategy) representing “an increasingly common departure from the more widespread crisp-and-clean stainless steel ferment,” Pickard explains.

Meanwhile, other producers are employing wild yeast and extended lees contact to “coax out the variety’s salty, flinty minerality while still celebrating its inherently bright fruit and zippy freshness,” she notes.

Clos Henri 2024 Otira Glacial Stones Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)

There’s a gentleness to this single site Sauvignon, from a top French-owned biodynamic estate. It’s concentrated, flavorsome, but never forced or over-made. There’s pronounced minerality—like stones, shells and fresh cut meadow crunching underfoot—and salted margarita vibes, with pineapple rind garnish. Bright, tangy acidity and herb-and-citrus flavors aren’t shy on the palate, but they never shout their presence. Mouth-watering and textural enough to be food friendly; the mineral core anchors a ship of flavor. Editor’s Choice. 95 Points — Christina Pickard

$23 Saratoga Wine

Greywacke 2023 Wild Sauvignon Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)

Anything with “wild” in the name indicates a wine that walks to its own beat, which Greywacke’s now iconic wine certainly does. No green veg-and-fruit combo typically found in Marlborough’s famed Sauvy B style here. Instead, a great swathe of rich, reductive characters appear from first inhale: toasted cashews, matchstick and grilled lemons. It’s waxy, salty and evocative. The palate is all about texture, rich and creamy but never heavy, thanks to permeating acidity that keeps the mouth watering. Sip beside grilled mushrooms or asparagus risotto. 95 Points — C.P.

$25 Total Wine & More

Te Mata 2023 Cape Crest Sauvignon Blanc (Hawke’s Bay)

Made with a touch of Semillon and Sauvignon Gris, this oak-aged Sauvy B is made more in the white Bordeaux vein than the Marlborough one. The nose shows flinty, toasty reductive notes. But the winemaking technique can’t fully obscure SB’s bombastic nature. There’s lemon-lime, pink grapefruit and pineapple with seashells, fennel fronds and summer herbs on the fringes; lovely richness and concentration on the palate, with salted grapefruit acidity and structure to make this a food friendly drop now, but one with the potential to age for several more years. 93 Points — C.P.

$38 Zachys

California

California’s expansive wine regions offer a tapestry of Sauvignon Blanc styles, reflecting the Golden State’s varied climates and winemaking philosophies. From the foggy valleys of Mendocino to the sun-drenched vineyards of Napa, Sauvignon Blanc can thrive in California’s diverse terroirs.

“There is a thumbprint being shown region by region in California,” says Tonya Pitts, Wine Enthusiast writer at large and Californian wine reviewer. “It’s great to see the range of style and flavor profile vary by region.”

That regional character shines through in the selections below, with each bottle telling a slightly different story.

Saracina 2022 Lolonis Vineyard Sauvignon Blanc (Mendocino)

Aromatics of crisp citrus, juicy melon, and dried herbs cascade across the nose. The mouthfeel is fresh with high energy, saline, and mouth-watering lime blossoms, White grapefruit, and fennel, creating a need to experience the concentrated bounty of this luminous nod to Summer. The Lolonis Vineyard is a prized site that has been my favorite for many years. It is a captivating expression of old vine Sauvignon Blanc. Pair with steamed mussels, garlic aioli. 96 Points — Tonya Pitts

$43 Flatiron Wines & Spirits

The Setting Wines 2024 Sauvignon Blanc (Sonoma County)

This richer-styled Sauvignon Blanc delivers focused aromas of ripe melon, candied ginger, pithy citrus and a whisper of flint on the nose, giving way to decadent flavors of grapefruit, honeydew, white flowers and chalky minerality on the palate before a lifted, acid-driven finish with echoes of orange blossom and sunshine. Editor’s Choice. 94 Points — Tom R. Capo

$55 The Setting

Larkmead 2023 Lillie Sauvignon Blanc (Napa Valley)

Succulent and fresh simultaneously, this 100% Sauvignon Blanc lifts from the glass with aromas of honeysuckle and citrus blossom, carrying into flavors of citrus pith, and spices. Mineral freshness completes the wine. 93 Points —Elaine Chukan Brown

$75 Larkmead

Italy

The foothills of the Alps produce some of the most unexpectedly stunning Sauvignon Blancs in the world. In Italy’s Alto Adige region, specifically, elevation and diurnal shifts give wines a clarity that’s both refreshing and powerful. 

Jeff Porter, Wine Enthusiast’s Northern Italian wine reviewer, sums up what makes these bottlings so great: “These wines are driven by their mountain extremes—warm days and cool nights—so ample aromas and flavors but with vibrant acidity and stony minerality.”

There’s less of the herbal edge typical of other regions, and more of a citrus-bright profile accented by alpine herbs. “Their palate tends to be broad but with electric acidity, so the wine fills the mouth but then zips like lightning,” Porter adds.

Cantina Terlano 2023 Quarz Sauvignon Blanc (Alto Adige Terlano)

If you think you know Sauvignon Blanc, this wine might flip your script. It’s got that classic grapefruit and herbs thing going on, but then it goes somewhere entirely different. There’s this wild smoky note that feels like early morning fog rolling over stone fruit orchards. The texture is what really gets you though – it’s got weight but floats across your palate like morning mist. Not trying to be Loire or New Zealand, just confidently doing its own Alto Adige thing. Pure mountain energy in a bottle. Drink Now – 2045. Cellar Selection. 95 Points — Jeff Porter

$90 B21

Luigino Balozio 2019 Sauvignon Cuar Sauvignon Blanc (Friuli Colli Orientali)

A kaleidoscope of aromas flows from this glass of this unique and delicious Sauvignon Blanc. Aromas of salted limes, sage, white flowers, sweet corn and green melon swirl about, a captivating nose, that is hard to put down. Tart and vigorous on the palate with a zippy energy that brightens that palate as green herbs, key lime and lemon all lead down to a path of sea salt and crushed stones on the vibrant finish. Buy a few and put at least one away, you will be pleasantly surprised by how well it will age. 93 Points — J.P.

$44 Liquor Express

Tenuta San Leonardo 2024 Vette Sauvignon Blanc (Vigneti delle Dolomiti)

Like hiking through an alpine meadow with a basket of citrus. The freshness hits different here, more crystalline than crisp. This is the wine equivalent of that perfect summer day when everything just feels right. Grilled shrimp would make it even better. 91 Points — J.P.

$26 Zachys

Bordeaux

While Bordeaux is perhaps best known for its reds, its whites—especially those from Pessac-Léognan and select parts of the Graves—offer some of the world’s most elegant expressions of Sauvignon Blanc. Often blended with Sémillon and aged in oak, these wines have the ability to show texture and gravitas.

There are bottles whose status and cost reflect that gravitas, but the famed region actually offers incredible diversity. “The range is wide, from wines under $15 to some sold for more than $200, so there is plenty of choice,” says Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large and Bordelais Wine Reviewer Roger Voss. “From crisp, young wines, dominated by herbal and citrus flavors to serious wood-aged wine that will last for many years.”

Château Margaux 2022 Pavillon Blanc de Château Margaux (Bordeaux Blanc)

This is a dense wine with richness and a tropical fruit aroma. Comes from the earliest harvest ever for this estate’s white grapes. There is a tantalizing grapefruit edge that shows the essence of the wine’s Sauvignon Blanc. Drink from 2026. 94 Points — Roger Voss

$420 Wine.com

Château la Louvière 2023 Pessac-Léognan

Pure, crisp Sauvignon Blanc, this wine shows a fine balance between herbal and tropical fruit flavors. Its quince and honey aroma and vivid grapefruit flavor are fresh and deliciously fruity. Drink from 2026. 92 Points — R.V.

$39 Millesima

Chateau Langlet 2024 Graves

A ripe, full wine with a fine balance between acidity and ripe tropical flavors. A mown grass aroma and a tight texture give the wine a fresh edge and aging potential. Drink from 2026. Editors’ Choice. 90 Points — R.V.

$26 Wine.com

Sancerre

“Sancerre is the paragon to which all Sauvignon Blanc on the planet is inevitably compared,” says Reggie Solomon, Wine Enthusiast writer at large and reviewer of wines from the Loire Valley.

While traditionally associated with grassy and herbal notes, Solomon points out that these wines have “become fuller and more robust in the last decade while still retaining attractive acidity and minerality.”

The best bottles show Sauvignon Blanc’s potential for elegance and complexity. Some see lees aging and even subtle oak, elevating gently ripened fruit to a new level.

And while Sancerre dominates wine lists, Solomon reminds us not to sleep on neighbors like Menetou-Salon and Pouilly-Fumé, which “offer value with similar depth of flavor” and definitely deserve attention.

Maison de Madeleine 2023 Sancerre Caillottes Sauvignon Blanc (Sancerre)

This gorgeous Sancerre is a chameleon in a glass whose character flips on the palate while remaining entirely delicious. Intense golden-fruit aromas of baked yellow apple and ripe Anjou pear surrender to brioche, bread dough, toast, bruised honeysuckle blossom and orange zest on the nose. The palate is creamy and delicious with yeasty elements bending a knee to ripe orchard fruit that asserts its hegemony while acknowledging its depth-inducing sidekick.  94 Points — Reggie Solomon

$33 The Wine Rack

Domaine Gérard Fiou 2023 Le Grand Roc Sauvignon Blanc (Sancerre)

This Sancerre is gorgeously complex. Gently-layered aromas of orange zest, ripe golden apple, dried mushroom and dew-dusted forest floor entice the nose that sees added notes of hay. Pure, elegant fruit is perfectly balanced with a supportive layer of acidity with well-integrated flavors that shine in the glass. Editor’s Choice. 94 Points — R.S.

$40 Sokolin

title: How to Serve Your Wine at the Right Temperature This Summer
summary:
From reds to whites and rosés, here's a complete guide to finding the right wine temp on the hottest of days.

The post How to Serve Your Wine at the Right Temperature This Summer appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Hitting the right wine serving temperature is actually a lot harder than it seems. Even some restaurants—where wine is ostensibly someone’s job—can struggle to hit the right service temp, sending out reds that have been baking in a warm “cellar” or whites so cold they feel like drinking hard seltzer. 

At home, many of us are working with whatever wine storage setup the kitchen counter and fridge can offer. Sure, it does the trick, but during the high heat of the summer months, chances are you probably want whatever you’re drinking to err on the side of chilled, at least to some degree. 

Don’t stress: we’re here to help with your summer wine service needs. Whether you’re drinking reds, whites, or rosés, we’ve compiled a guide to finding the right wine temp on the hottest of days.

Red Wine

The dusty old rule of serving reds at “room temperature” is somewhat misleading in the modern day. “That guideline goes back to European homes of the 18th and 19th centuries, which didn’t have good insulation or central heating,” says Fred Swan, professor of wine studies at Delta College. “60˚ F was common room temperature. Today, most people keep their homes from the mid-60s to the low-70s. That’s way too warm for any wine.” 

He also points to rising alcohol levels in today’s red wines, which he notes turn “boozy and gooey” when they’re served too warm. So, no matter the season, chances are your red wines are being served way too warm.

The answer isn’t to suddenly serve all red wines chilled, but to think in terms of weight and structure. A good rule of thumb is the lighter, the cooler. Fresh, low-tannin reds like Gamay, Frappato, or delicate Pinot Noir can benefit from a bit more chill. Think around 55–60˚ F, which is cool enough to lift the freshness without flattening the wine. A short fridge stint—say, around 20 minutes—is usually enough to get a lighter-bodied red into the sweet spot. 

Plusher reds like Cabernet, Syrah, and full-bodied blends can take a bit of cooling, but not so much that tannins become aggressive and complexity gets muted. Ideally, these should be served at 60–65˚ F. During the summer months, maybe even a touch cooler since they will lose their chill more quickly. If your house runs on the warmer side, a brief stay of 10 to 15 minutes in the fridge can help bring some vibrancy back to a heavier style red. 

“It’s not about serving reds warm or cool,” says Serge Doré, founder and president of import company Serge Doré Selections. “It’s about protecting their balance.”

Marshall Tilden III, president of Wine Express & Education at Wine Enthusiast, recommends starting any bottle a little bit cooler than you think you need to, since wines warm up much faster in the heat. It’s also worth keeping the bottle in a chiller and opting for more frequent, lighter pours to keep things at a nice temperature for every sip.

“If you don’t have a wine cellar or wine fridge set to a specific temperature, your best bet is to pop it in the fridge for a little bit,” says Autumn Gilliam, wine educator and owner of The Adventurous Sip. She notes that even a quick 15-minute spell can make a meaningful difference. 

IN THE SHOP

In Stock | $599

Shop Now

Tilden has a 20/20 rule: “If red wine is sitting out in the open, you want to put it in the fridge for 20 minutes. If white wine is in the fridge, you want to take it out 20 minutes before serving.”

White Wine

White wines usually end up at the other end of the spectrum. “People probably serve most whites and rosés too cold, pouring straight out of the refrigerator,” says Swan. “That’s going to hide a lot of the fruit and may also make oaked wines seem bitter.” 

Your kitchen fridge typically runs pretty cold, around 35–38˚ F, for food safety reasons. A calibrated wine fridge is noticeably warmer—about 45–55˚ F. So wines pulled from next to the ketchup bottle may be refreshing but not exactly interesting.

As with reds, style also matters here. “A crisp Sauvignon Blanc can be served quite cool to emphasize freshness,” says Doré. “But a richer white like a Meursault or a white Rhône will show far better a few degrees warmer, where texture, depth, and oak integration can emerge.”

Tilden suggests that anything below the low 40s is too cold for most still whites, but adds a caveat for the summer months: “You may need to chill it down to the high 30s so that by the time it gets in your glass and that outside warm air hits the wine, you can enjoy it at the perfect serving temp.” 

If you need to chill a wine quickly, a 50/50 ice-and-water bath works faster and gives you more control. From room temperature, that can take as little as 10 minutes for a red and about 20 minutes for a white. If you’re aiming for even colder—say, a brisk high 30s with the idea that the wine will warm up quickly once outside in the heat—plan on closer to 20 to 30 minutes.

In Stock | $40

Shop Now

Rosé

There’s a reason rosé gets its own season: it’s just that quaffable, especially in warm weather. But it’s also the wine most likely to get overcorrected in the name of crushability. 

“If you put [rosé] in an ice bucket for 30 minutes or more, you will have completely suffocated it,” says Doré. “The aromatics will be muted, textures lighten, and the wine loses its ability to express nuance. What you’re left with is freshness—but without personality.”

Gilliam suggests 45–55˚ F for rosé. From room temperature, it should take rosé about 20 minutes in the fridge or 10 to 15 minutes in an ice-and-water bath. If your bottle is coming straight from a cold kitchen fridge, let it sit out for 10 to 20 minutes before serving. This should ensure it stays crisp and refreshing without dulling the fruit and aromatics.  

Style still plays a role, but even that is a suggestion, not a commandment. “I can’t say that I have seen these wines being overchilled as a trend, but if you enjoy it cooler than standard on a hot summer day, then go for it,” says Tilden. “However you enjoy it is the right serving temp.”

It’s a thought that bears repeating: there is no wrong way to drink wine you enjoy. Swan notes that even dropping an ice cube into a glass is more acceptable than some would have you believe. “I’ve seen plenty of Europeans do that during summer lunches al fresco. And I have it from reliable sources that Jacques Pépin and Robert Mondavi have done it on occasion,” he says. “The important thing is enjoying the wine and the experience.”

If you do want to take some of the guesswork out on a more permanent basis, investing in a dedicated wine storage solution is worth considering. “For everyday wine drinkers, a standard wine fridge is great,” notes Gilliam. “Keep it set at 55˚ F and everything will be close to proper temperature.” 

For households that regularly keep all types of wine on hand, Swan recommends a dual-zone cooler with one zone set at 50–52˚ F for lighter whites and chillable reds, and another at 55–57˚ F for fuller whites and bigger reds. Not a requirement, of course, but it’ll help you get the right temp for every bottle—no extra chilling necessary.

title: The Dreamiest Winery Guest Houses to Book Around the World
summary:
From a grand home in a Napa vineyard to an 18th-century villa in Spain, here's where you can turn wine country living fantasies into reality.

The post The Dreamiest Winery Guest Houses to Book Around the World appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Wine grapes are grown in some of the world’s most beautiful places: the rolling hills of Tuscany, the flats and slopes of the Napa Valley, and under the shadows of mountains in South Africa’s southern coast.

All are well worth a visit for wine lovers. But instead of stopping into a winery for a tasting or a dinner before trekking back to a hotel, why not stay a while? Wineries across the globe boast impressive, elegant homes that are situated directly in vineyards or on estate grounds. 

Wake up and enjoy coffee while overlooking the fields from a well-appointed cottage in the Napa Valley. Meander through vines of Cabernet in the French countryside before retiring in a castle suite for the evening. Or, take in the sunset beside a farmhouse in Portugal’s Douro Valley. No matter your accommodation preference, there’s a wine country guest house to fulfill your wildest dreams.

So, if you’re seeking a distinctly immersive experience, book a stay at one of these beautiful winery guest houses now.

Casa Mon

 Piedmont

In Gaia Gaja’s free time—which is already limited, considering she runs some of the most pedigreed wineries in Italy—she carefully and thoughtfully restored a web of Medieval buildings in the center of Monforte d’Alba, Piemonte and turned them into Casa Mon. The six-bedroom home overlooks the vineyards of Barolo and Barbaresco. Check in, spend your afternoon poolside under a bell tower built in the 1400s, then feel free to pick through the wine list, which includes selections from the Gaja family’s personal cellar. Gaja has faithfully restored the space so all six bedrooms are well-appointed and have chic, design-forward details (plus, each one overlooks rolling Piemonte vineyards). A basement spa will help you relax after a tough day of tasting.

Skipstone Winery

Sonoma

The residence at Skipstone is an 8,000-square-foot chalet nestled on a Sonoma hillside. Guests can wake up early to jog the 27 acres of biodynamic and organic vines, and end the day watching the sunset around the property’s giant fire pit. Six indoor fireplaces and five ensuite bedrooms are spread through the house (including one in a soaring turret). Guests have access to the 3,000-bottle private cellar, which includes the winery’s Bordeaux-style blends, and get priority access to local restaurants. (Fancy a dinner at three-Michelin-starred SingleThread?) If you’re looking to eat on-premise, fresh eggs from the home’s chicken coop will make a mean breakfast.

Finca Viladellops

Catalunya

Perched atop a hill in Catalunya, Finca Viladellops has spruced up rural houses around the winery’s land and turned them into charming rentals surrounded by olive groves and almond trees. Rentable options include beautifully restored 11th-century farmhouses and an 18th-century villa. These original properties have retained their old world charm, albeit with more modern furnishings and amenities. All rentals come complete with private pools, large kitchens, and plenty of space for a crowd, and the prime Penedès location puts you within a ten-to-twenty minute drive of most of the region’s best wineries.

Tenuta CastelGiocondo

Tuscany

This charming countryside home in the medieval village of Castelgiocondo is the epitome of Tuscan charm. Wild Mediterranean herbs grow around the property. Four-post beds are surrounded by floaty white curtains. As if the Instagram appeal wasn’t enough, there’s plenty of history to the space—Tenuta CastelGiocando was one of the first estates to produce Brunello di Montalcino in the 19th century.

Planeta

Sicily

Planeta, one of Sicily’s most prominent wineries, operates a network of charming homes and estates around the island with different stylistic expressions. In the Baroque city of Noto (of White Lotus fame), small rural one- and two-bedroom homes done up in earth tones and soft wood accents dot the vineyards—perfect for a couple’s getaway. Meanwhile over in Sambuca di Sicilia, an “Artist’s House” is full of creative elements such as colorful textiles andItalian Renaissance-style art. It’s right in the center of the city, so you can trot into town and have a spritz or some arancini.

La Ghiandaia

Prosecco

La Ghiandaia’s early-1900s farmhouse was carefully restored by L’Antica Querica, an organic winery and olive farm in the hills of Prosecco Superiore DOCG (an hour outside Venice). Gardens, ancient oak trees, and the home’s natural modern aesthetic are the big draw, as are the outdoor hot tubs, sauna, and Turkish bath. The winery itself is a short hike away, so you can head there for tastings and skip the drive home. Or, use the space as a base for exploring the Prosecco Route.

Gibbston Valley Lodge & Spa

Central Otago

Gibbston Valley’s posh vineyard villas are dotted between rows of Pinot Noir vines in Central Otago, just 25 minutes from Queenstown. Vineyard-facing patios are perfect for perching with a glass of wine while soaking in the views. An on-site gym plus fireplaces, hot tubs, and kitchens in every villa combine the amenities of a hotel with the privacy of a vacation rental. Break up tastings by biking up the Kawarau River, which skirts the property.

Merry Edwards

Russian River Valley

The charms of this peaceful Russian River retreat are obvious.The sprawling grounds and cottage airs of Maefield Vineyard House feel like summer camp. The four-bedroom house boasts wood paneled walls and a lived-in charm with a stand-alone fireplace and spectacular views from multiple decks. There’s enough room for the entire family (or a bachelorette troupe), outdoor dining space, and a sauna. Every stay includes a bottle of Sauvignon Blanc and a tasting at the winery.

Alexandria Nicole Cellars

Columbia River AVA

Wake up in one of Alexandria Nicole Cellars’ tiny homes, walk outside, and let the Columbia River landscapes greet you. Rural is the operative word here—houses are forty miles from the nearest restaurant. Stock up on fresh produce from the region, grab some wine from the winery, turn off notifications, disconnect, and fully immerse yourself in the natural scenery. Homes are named after wines—Affinity Albariňo, Gravity Merlot—and are decked out with big beds, big patios, and barbecues.

Manor House B&B

Texas Hill Country

Despite its Hill Country zip code, Hessian Hof’s Fredericksburg bed-and-breakfast has a heavy European accent. Each one of the four cottages on the property are themed after a different country. The Paris cottage is decorated with a French Provencal flair, while the Messina cottage nods to the Bonarrigos’ Sicilian history with colorful textiles and a classic four poster bed. A German cottage contains mementos from Merrill Bonarrigo’s ancestors, while the President’s cottage is all American with a leather sofa and traditional dark wood furnishing. All stays include a complementary bottle of sparkling wine and a tasting at the Texas winery.

Dogpoint

Marlborough

True New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc fans should make a pilgrimage to Marlborough’s Dog Point Vineyard and book a stay at the French barn. The two-bedroom villa in Omaka Valley is circled by vineyards and decked out with clawfoot tubs, rustic, chic furnishings and, of course, incredible views.

Quinta do Vallado

Douro Valley

Quina do Vallado’s suites are so isolated it’s almost a mile hike to the main building (though the winery is happy to lend you one of their electric buggies). The property is a quiet reprieve from Porto’s hustle and bustle. The suites, seated atop of Douro’s steep slopes, are surrounded by terraced vineyards and Corgo River, offering spectacular panoramas no matter which way you look. Every freestanding cottage has a fireplace, a balcony, and a plunge pool overlooking the Douro River.

Castello del Trebbio

Tuscany

Also under the Tuscan sun: Castello del Trebbio’s agriturismo farm stays. Historic villas and apartments are available, all of which offer access to swimming pools (there are three on site) and peaceful outdoor sitting areas that either overlook the vineyards, 12th-century castle, or the bucolic landscape. The recently renovated spaces maintain the rustic farmhouse vibe of the property and can accommodate a range of party sizes. The smallest apartment features three beds, while the largest villa can accommodate up to 19 beds, allowing for a large array of sleeping arrangements for a wide range of occasions.

  • The cocktail scene in Warsaw is electric—and totally worth planning a trip around.
  • And then there’s Vancouver, which is full of world-class bars and excellent dining.

title: Marselan Is About to Have a Hot Grape Summer
summary:
Marselan, a heat-loving cross between Cabernet Sauvignon and Grenache, is seducing winemakers around the globe.

The post Marselan Is About to Have a Hot Grape Summer appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Named for the coastal Languedoc town in which it was bred, Marselan is a grape variety born in 1961 as a cross between Cabernet Sauvignon and Grenache. As is not always the case with the offspring of intentional grape crossings (see: Pinotage, Müller-Thurgau, etc.), Marselan actually exhibits the best qualities of both of its parents.

“You can really taste that lineage,” says Ignacio Schindler, owner of Argentina Wine Imports. “It inherits that vibrant, red fruit profile from the Grenache, but the Cabernet steps in to provide an elegant structure and a bold, sophisticated finish,” he says. “I think of Marselan as the natural go-between for people who enjoy fresh and fruity wines, and those who steer toward the heavier reds.”

In addition to its potential for bridging the gap between differing camps of wine drinkers, winemakers also tend to describe Marselan wines with reverent, sensual language, giving it some serious main character energy.

“It’s very sexy, very expressive, fruit-forward, and spicy like Grenache,” says Christian Wylie, managing director at Uruguay’s Bodega Garzón, “where the Cab element is a little more shy, but when that backbone walks in you have a whole different player.” 

Yan Song, owner of LongTing Vineyard in China, where Marselan is making especially bold strokes, agrees. “It has a very attractive aroma,” she says, “and an elegant, juicy body with nice tannins that are very soft and silky.”

If Marselan is already sounding like a regular seductress in a glass, it doesn’t hurt that she’s more eye-catching than either of the parent grapes here, with a vivid purple hue bordering on magenta. But Marselan is much more than a cute look and a tasty bottle. From South America to China and just about everywhere in between—including its native France—Marselan is proving itself as a protagonist grape that is going to play an even bigger role in the steamy years to come.

Marselan Is Getting Hot, Literally

If you’re wondering why this apparently va-va-voom grape dynamo has been flying under the radar in the 60-plus years since its birth, the cross that resulted in Marselan was initially deemed somewhat unsuccessful. 

According to Wine Grapes: A complete guide to 1368 vine varieties, including their origins and flavors (Robinson, Harding, Vouillamoz), the specific goal in Marselan’s development was to create a high-yielding grape of moderate quality and a large berry structure. Its resulting small berries, however, meant that it wasn’t immediately heralded as the next coming of Cabernet.

Marselan was only released as a commercially viable wine variety in 1990, when ever-warming summers around the world saw the need for more disease-resistant grapes. Once planted, initially in France and Spain, and then beyond, Marselan delivered on a different promise. “It’s the most beloved grape for the vineyard workers,” says Wylie. “It’s becoming the go-to red variety for climate change, in that it is very resistant to both drought as well as floods, and has a loose structure in its bunch,” he says, allowing for good air flow and staving off mildew and botrytis. 

To underscore the importance of this, Marselan was recently added among the permissible red grape varieties specifically to help deal with climate change in Bordeaux AOC. A quicker ripening period than Cabernet Sauvignon also means that there’s less threat of grape damage from late-season storms.

Around the World with Marselan

In France, Marselan is becoming a rising star in its ancestral homeland, in the price-friendly IGP Pays d’Oc. In nearby Bordeaux producers are only allowed to use up to 10% of Marselan or other auxiliary varieties in a Bordeaux AOC or Bordeaux Supérieur AOC blend, however producers in the region are nonetheless experimenting with 100% Marselan releases.

An early adopter of Marselan, Château de Respide in Graves planted a single hectare of the variety in 2017 in anticipation of hotter and hotter summers. “Marselan is clearly able to withstand heat and drought and has reached perfect levels of maturity in certain vintages,” says Amélie Chatin, the château’s owner, which inspired varietal bottlings of Marselan beginning in 2022, which were named “Renavida” or “rebirth” in the Occitan language. 

“In the Bordelais landscape a 100% Marselan wine is clearly unique by the aromatic spiciness and the fruit-dominated palate,” says Chatin, “two characteristics which are never present with such intensity in Bordeaux wines.”

In Central and South America, Marselan has been cropping up wherever wine is produced, including up-and-coming Bolivia and Brazil. From Mexico, Vos Selections (the recent victors of the Supreme Court’s ruling on tariffs) is importing a 100% Marselan from El Bajío that is made exclusively for the U.S. market. 

According to Uruguay’s Instituto Nacional de Vitivinicultura’s (INAVI) interactive vineyard georeferencing module, plantings of Marselan in Uruguay are on par with another rising star from the region: Cabernet Franc. And Argentinean Marselan is also starting to make its way to the U.S. as a Malbec alternative. “Since recently incorporating a Marselan into my portfolio this year, the feedback has been compelling,” says Schindler. “I think it’s resonating with U.S. drinkers because they are increasingly looking for ‘discovery’ wines—bottles that tell a new story but still deliver a high-quality, balanced experience.”

China Makes Marselan a Signature

This “discovery” element of Marselan is also helping to tell the story of Chinese wine in a big way. “Marselan was introduced as an experimental variety to the Sino-French Demonstration Vineyard in 2001,” says Demei Li, director of Enology Engineering in Beijing University

of Agriculture, along with other varieties, “as a formal cooperation project between the two countries’ ministries of agriculture,” an effort to bring attention to China’s potential as a wine producing country. From the first vintage in 2003, “It was of such high quality, the yield was higher, and it showed moderate growth vigor in the vineyard without requiring too much summer pruning, and had good disease resistance compared with Cabernet Sauvignon,” says Li, which was initially planted in the largest volume in China.

As Marselan isn’t as widely known as other international grapes that were also planted, it is also seen as an opportunity for winemakers in China to claim the grape as a signature for the country. “There’s an opportunity for Marselan to become for China what Shiraz is for Australia, or Malbec for Argentina,” says Song. With a variety of microclimates in the Chinese wine landscape, Marselan is also demonstrating versatility, with its ability to adapt to different terroirs in both coastal and inland regions.

China has long been one of the biggest consumers of Bordeaux wines worldwide, but in terms of putting their own stamp on the industry, the championing of Marselan is also a savvy move. “With a Chinese Cabernet Sauvignon, maybe the world consumer is going to compare it with a French, Italian, or American Cabernet,” says Song, “but with Marselan we’re giving consumers something special, with a Chinese identity.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Bodega Garzón 2023 Reserva Marselan (Maldonado)

Marselan, a cross between Cabernet Sauvignon and Grenache, was bred in France and has made its way to emerging wine regions like Uruguay. This wine offers sweet blackberry aromas that fill the nose. A hint of nutmeg complements the blend of dark fruit, cocoa beans, and herbs on the bold palate, where balanced acidity enhances the flavors. 91 Points — Jesica Vargas

$18 Saratoga Wine

Bodegas Neleman 2022 Hey You! Red Blend (Valencia)

This wine has a restrained power that you can feel if you give it the opportunity. Fresh violet blossom, candied red cherry and raspberry puree converge on the nose and moonlight with toasted almond slivers. A light layer of blueberry-tinged smokiness infuses the palate and raises red cherry that holds your hand on the finish. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — Reggie Solomon

$17 Toast Wines

Recanati 2020 Reserve Marselan (Galilee)

This deep red-violet wine has a nose of blackberry, raspberry and dried lavender. A sheath of velvety tannins encases a strong vein of acidity and pomegranate, raspberry, cocoa powder, crumbled sage and orange blossom flavors. 92 Points — Mike DeSimone

$43 Kosher Wine

title: The Best Long Island Wineries to Visit Right Now
summary:
In New York State, Long Island is just hitting its stride as a wine region. Here are the top wineries to visit.

The post The Best Long Island Wineries to Visit Right Now appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
New York State is full of surprises. You know you’ve hit the rural bits of Long Island’s North Fork—and, therefore, wine country—when you see a giant inflatable strawberry jiggling in the wind, like the one outside Bayview Market & Farms in Riverhead. The hamlet of Flanders’s Big Duck, whose innards house a poultry store, serves as a similar landmark at the mouth of Long Island’s South Fork. They’re not official markers, of course, but there’s something delightful about a conspicuously oversized roadside attraction letting you know you’ve left the city far behind.

Comprising 1,400 square miles, Long Island is the nation’s largest and longest contiguous island. It consists, in large part, of sprawling suburbs and strip malls, but its East End, as it is known, is far more bucolic. Divided into the North Fork and South Fork by the Peconic Bay, this sliver of land birthed the region’s wine industry half a century ago.

Long Island celebrated a half century of winemaking in 2023. Fifty years may be a heartbeat in a global wine context, but it’s a profound milestone for a region like Long Island, which is, in many ways, just hitting its stride. Wine grape farming is improving. As some vines come of age, others are being dug up and replaced with more climate-suitable clones and varieties. Meanwhile, second-generation producers—alongside new, internationally experienced winemakers—are getting their hands dirty in Long Island’s sandy loam soils.

Combine that with a multitude of recently opened or renovated tasting room and food experiences, plus the ever-enticing beach-life vibes, and there’s no better time to acquaint—or reacquaint—yourself with New York’s second-largest wine-producing region.

As Wine Enthusiast’s reviewer of wines from New York State, I hold Long Island near and dear to my heart. Below you’ll find my picks of some of Long Island’s top wineries, along with recent releases bound to delight even more than inflatable fruit and giant poultry.

The North Fork

Stretching from Riverhead to Orient Point, the North Fork contains more than 60 vineyards spread across the nearly 30-mile-long stretch. The pastoral peninsula is dotted with farm stands and more than 100 active farms, which are a draw throughout the spring, summer, and fall harvest seasons. 

Paumanok Vineyards

Aquebogue

One of Long Island’s westernmost wineries, Paumanok Vineyards is also one of its most longstanding. Founded in 1983 by Ursula and Charles Massoud, who emigrated from Germany and Lebanon, respectively, Paumanok is now run by sons Kareem and Salim. A true family business, Kareem is winemaker and Salim the operation’s administrative manager. Charles and Ursula are still very involved in the winery’s key decision-making.

Paumanok’s winery and tasting room are housed in a renovated, turn-of-the-century barn clad in weatherboards. The interior is airy and calm, while the large deck provides expansive views of the vineyards, which parade over one of the few hills on the notoriously flat island. Paumanok hosts a sunset special of raw oysters, a Greek snack bar, and $8 glasses every Friday in the summer. But it’s also worth snagging a seat at one of the Grand Vintage tasting dinners. Like other long-established Long Island producers, Paumanok is known for holding back stocks of their highly cellar-worthy Merlot and Bordeaux blends, but it also makes an excellent Chenin Blanc, “minimalist” Cab Franc, plus glug-worthy aromatic whites like Albariño under the Palmer label.

In 2018, the Massouds purchased nearby Palmer Vineyards, where they operate a second tasting room that’s reminiscent of a traditional English pub, with live music performances and dinners featuring wood-fired pizzas. The Massouds’ third son, Nabeel, runs Fiocha Pizza in Mattituck, also known for its stellar wood-fired pizza.

Macari

Mattituck

Like Paumanok, Macari is a family affair. The winery began in 1995 when Joseph Macari Jr. and his father first planted vines on the 500 acres of fallow land—formerly a potato farm—that Joseph Sr. had purchased in the 1960s. Today, Joseph Jr. and his wife Alexandra still helm the ship, but daughter Gabriella and son Joseph M. are also intimately involved, the former as Director of Operations and the latter as Head of Viticulture.

Macari has long been a leader on the viticulture front, employing organic and biodynamic techniques and omitting herbicides. In 2020, Macari brought in a new winemaker, Byron Elmendorf, who has taken its operation in an exciting direction, one that sees more wild ferments, lees aging and a general uptick in experimentation and creativity.

Despite having to navigate the notoriously strict regulations surrounding food service from both New York State and Southold township (where around 75% of the North Fork’s wineries are located), Macari offers a multitude of events that feature visiting chefs like Lauren Lombardi, whose beautifully prepared and ultra fresh seasonal dishes are worth the trip alone.

In 2022, Macari renovated and re-launched its tasting room in nearby Cutchogue as Meadowlark. With a beautifully designed wine bar, events space and a range of wines, Meadowlark seems set to become one of the North Fork’s top wine and wedding destinations.

Rose Hill Vineyards

Mattituck

Rose Hill is one of the new(ish) kids on the North Fork block, having been purchased by Randy and Barbara Frankel in 2017 and fully renovated and rebranded in 2021. But in its former guise as Shinn Estate, this Mattituck-based winery was one of the first to establish a connection with New York City’s restaurant scene. These days the wines are sold almost entirely from the tasting room, but it’s worth the in-person visit. Rose Hill boasts one of Long Island’s most beautifully designed wine spaces. Beside the classic weatherboard farmhouse that serves as a four bedroom inn, there’s a welcoming vine-side patio, a shop selling local goods as well as wine, and a gorgeous boho-chic tasting room offering modern, delicate wines made by longtime winemaker Patrick Caserta (try the Brut Rosé to complete the classy feel). Pair them with filling “light bites” like warm pretzel batons, sliders with prosciutto, burrata, and arugula, and whipped ricotta and crostini.

Note: Rose Hill’s sister establishments—both as thoughtfully designed as Rose is—are also worth checking out. The focus of Croteaux in Southold is exclusively on rosé, served from a space that feels simultaneously classic Long Island and also like something out of the French Riviera, with lobster sliders and croque monsieur to pair. Meanwhile, Main + Mill in Jamesport is designed with 1920s Hollywood glamour in mind, with an expansive patio for summer sipping and a cozy tavern-style space indoors with a crackling fire to wile the winter blues away. The wines, while fairly classic in style (all made by Caserta), pair with vintage dishes like shrimp cocktail, pigs in a blanket, and tater tots. There’s even a Shirley Temple on the menu for the little ones, making this a family-friendly stop.

Lieb Cellars

Cutchogue

Perched less than a mile from the Long Island Sound, Lieb Cellars makes remarkably saline winesthat speak of their maritime surroundings. This is thanks both to the careful farming of longtime vineyard foreman Ildo Vasquez, and to Lieb’s Aussie-born winemaker Russell Hearn. In 2023, Hearn co-purchased Lieb from its former owners and the cozy-yet-classy tasting room now pours wines from both Lieb and Hearn’s other label, Suhru Wines. Both can be enjoyed with live music, via a themed masterclass, or with freshly shucked local oysters on Friday oyster nights.

Bedell Cellars

Cutchogue

The tasting room at Bedell Cellars has a bright and airy modern appeal that pairs with its clean, textural, food-friendly wines. This is one of Long Island’s longest-standing wineries, dating back to 1980 (with a change in ownership in 2000). Long Island wine pioneer Richard Olsen-Harbich spearheaded Bedell’s winemaking for over 40 years before officially handing the reins over to Marin Brennan in 2024. Brennan has continued the winery’s spirit of creativity and penchant for saline-streaked aromatic whites. All fermentations are spontaneous, or kickstarted with self-foraged botanicals to capture the essence of Long Island terroir.

Corey Creek is Bedell’s second label, also made by Brennan, who launched the new range in 2021. At Corey Creek’s Tap Room, the vibe is more artisanal, with a chic beach hut feel and live music on the front porch overlooking the vineyards.

Lenz

Peconic

Wedged between Bedell and Corey Creek is Lenz, Long Island’s second-oldest winery. Founded in 1978, the winery has been owned by Peter and Deborah Carroll since 1988. In that time, Lenz has had just one vineyard manager, Sam McCullough, and two winemakers: Eric Fry, who retired after 30 years in 2018, and Thomas Spotteck, who was Fry’s assistant winemaker. Lenz is best known for holding back its reds for at least four to five years before release, and for its aromatic Alsatian varieties like Gewürztraminer and Pinot Gris.

The tasting room, housed in an old barn surrounded by some of the region’s oldest vines, is quintessential North Fork. There’s a range of pre-prepared food and non-wine drink options, including cheese and charcuterie, cold brew, and local beer. Events throughout the year include “Dinner in the Vines” and pop-ups like local dumplings and wine.

The South Fork

A 75-minute drive (or 90 minutes via car ferry, which is highly recommended) from Greenport on the North Fork, Long Island’s South Fork may be equally beautiful, but it’s otherwise an entirely different scene. This part of Long Island is home to the Hamptons, the famed coastal playground for New York’s wealthiest inhabitants. There are just three wineries here, each with its own vibe.

Wölffer

Sagaponack

Perhaps nowhere embodies the Hamptons more than Wölffer Estate, one of Long Island’s busiest, buzziest tasting rooms. High-end SUVs regularly drop gaggles of young, well-groomed guests outside the steps of the Provençal chateau-inspired estate, where they sip dry rosé beside sweeping vineyard vistas.

Wölffer’s Summer in a Bottle rosé, which helped popularize the dry, southern French style now ubiquitous in America—and of which the winery makes a staggering 125,000 cases a year—now comes in two cuvées, one made on Long Island and the other in the Côtes de Provence. (There’s also now a Summer in a Bottle Sauvignon Blanc and a non-alcoholic range, Spring in a Bottle). 

But look beyond the aspirational lifestyle branding and what you’ll find is a vast range of high-quality wines that range from $16 to $125 a bottle. The ability to juggle all ends of the quality spectrum is a tribute to Wölffer’s gregarious German-born winemaker, Roman Roth, who has clocked 34 vintages at the company and is a business partner.

Roth also has a private label, Grapes of Roth. Both labels can be tasted with small plate snacks at the tasting room or at Wölffer’s Wine Stand, just around the corner from the estate, on Montauk Highway.

Channing Daughters

Bridgehampton

Despite being less than a 10-minute drive from Wölffer, the understated and idiosyncratic Channing Daughters couldn’t be any more different. Here the focus is not on the small tasting room—charming though it may be—or on the simple outdoor set up of vine-side picnic tables and tents. The wines themselves are the main attraction. They are among the most colorful on Long Island. James Christopher Tracy, who has been the winemaker since the early 2000s and is now a business partner, is restlessly creative, crafting a dizzying range (over two dozen separate bottlings) of minimal-intervention, textural wines from a huge array of grape varieties, including some not seen anywhere else in New York. There’s a waxy, melon-y Tocai Fruilano, an array of aromatic field blends, and a golden, oxidative Ribolla Gialla, to name just a few. While some are more successful than others, each wine is distinctly different, expressing varietal, place and the winemaker’s intention.

FAQ

Are Long Island Wineries Open Year-Round?

Yes, although some tasting rooms limit hours during winter months. In the summer, some wineries close for weddings and other events. While a few still require reservations, most accept walk-ins. It’s always best to check a winery’s website or social media pages if you have your heart set on visiting. If you prefer to wing it, North Fork wineries in particular are close enough together that if one is shut, you’ll likely find another open nearby.

How Many Wineries Are on Long Island?

Long Island is home to around 60 wineries across over 3,000 acres of vineyards.

What Wine is Long Island Known For?

Traditionally, Merlot, Bordeaux blends (known in the U.S. as “Meritage”) and Chardonnay, along with dry rosé. But these days, Long Island’s varietal palate is broad.

The moderate-to-cool maritime climate makes low-alcohol wines that are naturally high in acidity with a distinctive saline thread. There are excellent examples of Alsatian and German whites like Pinot Blanc, Pinot Gris, and Gewurztraminer, with some Sauvignon Blanc (the most interesting spend time in old oak and on lees) and Albariño, the current “it” variety. For reds, Cabernet Franc is particularly exciting and ripens more consistently than northern New York regions. Northern Italian grapes like Teroldego and Lagrein also find a happy home on the East End.

What Is the Largest Winery on Long Island?

Pindar claims Long Island’s largest production, with 70,000 cases, of wines made in Long Island, but if Wölffer’s Summer in a Bottle Côtes de Provence label is included in the tally, it puts the South Fork winery in the lead.

Which Part of Long Island Has the Most Vineyards?

Most producers are located in the North Fork, between the towns of Riverhead and Greenport. There are just three wineries on the South Fork.

title: Somms Are Obsessing Over this Racy White Wine from Hungary
summary:
Despite its long history in Hungary’s Tokaj region, the white wine grape Furmint isn’t yet a household name in the U.S.—it soon will be.

The post Somms Are Obsessing Over this Racy White Wine from Hungary appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Can a wine you’ve never heard of have main character energy? Shannon Saulsbury thinks so.

“Drinking Furmint is like locking eyes with a complete stranger in a bar and getting goosebumps,” says the wine and sake program manager of Flagship Restaurant Group in Omaha, Nebraska. “I have a theory that Furmint could be the most popular grape in the world, if only I could get it into everyone’s glasses.”

Despite its long history in Hungary’s Tokaj region, the white wine grape Furmint isn’t a household name in the United States—at least, not yet. But the movement is underway. 

At bars and restaurants across the country, sommeliers and the people who drink with them swoon over dry Furmint’s racy acidity, minerally complexity, and food-friendly versatility. Some of the best bottles cost a fraction of comparable options from Burgundy or the Mosel, and their distinctive sense of place speaks to what modern consumers want from wine.

Hungarian winemakers are more than happy to meet the moment. With increased production and attention among forward-thinking producers, and a slow but steady rise in U.S. imports, dry Furmint is poised to go from industry darling to mainstream success. Will it make the leap? 

Dry Over Dessert

Rarely cultivated outside of central Europe, Furmint has been grown in Tokaj, Hungary, for millennia. It plays a starring role in Tokaj Aszú, the region’s legendary sweet wines that sell for thousands of dollars to private collectors and fine-dining beverage directors worldwide. 

As a varietal wine, however, dry Furmint is barely past legal drinking age. Over the last 25-odd years, amid climate change and declining global appetites for dessert wines, some Tokaj winemakers flipped the script and began bottling racy, bone-dry Furmints. As their skills grew, so did their audiences: Dry white wines now comprise some 70% of Tokaj production, according to Wines of Hungary. 

One of the first bottled dry Furmints came from Disznókő, a Tokaj winery, in 1992. Szepsy, another early adopter, bottled its initial dry Furmint in 2000. 

“This is the sort of dry wine that meets the demand of today’s customers,” says László Meszáros, director and winemaker of Disznókő. “It has high acidity, minerality, and it can pair easily with a lot of modern food. 

“But it’s still a difficult market for us,” Meszáros adds, noting how many dry white wines have better name recognition with global consumers. “Wine professionals, sommeliers, and collectors have a very strong interest, but Furmint isn’t well-known in many markets. It’s people who know wine very well who understand that there’s a depth to these dry Furmint wines.”

Making a Mark

Amy Mundwiler agrees. The national director of wine for Maple Hospitality Group has a dry Furmint in magnum on the list of the Chicago location of Maple & Ash steakhouse, but her team typically has to hand-sell it. “Guests are not familiar [with Furmint], we have to guide them to this wine,” she says. 

That isn’t always a bad thing, says Rowen McDermott, the owner of Binx, a buzzy bistro in Manhattan’s West Village. “I don’t think Furmint is the most well-known grape, but in a way that’s helpful.”

He uses Riesling to illustrate his point. The variety inspires industry insiders to wax poetic and create killer memes, but many U.S. consumers “think all Riesling is either going to be super dry with not a lot to it, or really sweet and honeysuckled,” McDermott says. Since fewer people in the U.S. are familiar with dry Furmint, there aren’t scores of preconceived notions to dispel.

Besides, once guests try Furmint, they’re impressed. “It has some of the characteristics of white Burgundy, but without the flabbier side of white Burgundy,” he says. “It’s sharp and precise with great texture and acidity, but still with a luscious background.”

It’s a relatively affordable wine to take a chance on, too. While many top-rated white Burgundies will run you $100 or more, I’ve reviewed bottles of 90-points-or-higher dry Furmint that cost less than $25.

“To get this level of complexity, vibrancy, and vigor in a white wine from France or Italy, you’d be paying double or triple in most cases, at a minimum,” Saulsbury says. 

She finds it more stylistically consistent than many mainstream white wine varieties. “It’s not like buying Chardonnay where you have to do the math on whether or not it’s going to be oaked or steely or buttery or Chablis-y. When you pick up a bottle of dry Furmint, you know you are going to get an incredible bottle of wine that’s an absolute steal.” 

It’s hard to think of a better way to make a first impression.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Disznókö 2022 Dry Furmint (Tokaji)

This deliciously tart Hungarian white wine has bright citrus aromas and flavors followed by a long, tart finish. Serve it as an aperitif or pair with grilled chicken, shellfish, and similarly light fare. 90 points — Emily Saladino

$22 Wine Spectrum

Disznókő 2022 Inspiration Dry Furmint-Hárslevelű (Tokaji)

There’s an entire orchard’s worth of aromas in this elegant dry Furmint, including squeezed tangerines and just-picked white peaches. The palate is tart and beautifully balanced, with citrus and minerally notes plus a long, tart finish. 91 points — E.S.

$26 Liquor Cave

Royal Tokaji 2019 SZT. Tamas Furmint Furmint (Tokaj)

Unpeeled hunks of ginger, whole cloves, and juicy pears headline the nose of this dry Furmint from a First Growth vineyard with clay and volcanic soils. The palate is beautifully dry and crisp, with fresh and stewed pears alongside tart lemon juice and rind. The long, citrus-forward finish will have you reaching for your next sip—and asking for a second glass—before you know it. 93 points — E.S.

$80 Westgate Wine

Oremus 2022 Mandolás Furmint (Tokaj)

With its beautifully textured palate and long, bright finish, this dry Furmint has ample food-pairing potential. Expect tart lemon and stone fruit aromas and flavors followed by a very long, citrus-forward finish. 94 points — E.S.

$40 Vino Nueva

title: The Best Pinot Grigios for Under $20
summary:
Pinot Grigio, also known as Pinot Gris, can be used to produce stunning, high-end wines. But it’s also responsible for these excellent under-$20 bottles.

The post The Best Pinot Grigios for Under $20 appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Hear us out: Pinot Grigio just might be the most underrated wine out there.

The crowd-pleasing crisp white wine has a strong presence on menus and in bottle shops around the world, so much so that it has become almost… ordinary. This ubiquity might make it seem less exciting than other white varietals, but when you look under the hood, it’s a real banger—especially when you factor in the cost-to-quality ratio. 

Looking for an outstanding white that’ll cost under $20? Pinot Grigio is your best friend.

A Wine of Many Faces

Pinot Grigio, also known as Pinot Gris, can be used to produce stunning, high-end bottles, but its true genius shines in affordable, quaffable, and blissfully simple wines. The anti-Chardonnay, Pinot Grigio almost never sees oak, delivering crisp, bracing acidity while veering toward steely, sharp minerality or toward tropical and stone fruit flavors.

“Pinot Grigio comes in many guises, from light and crisp to rich and bold,” says Jeff Porter, Wine Enthusiast writer at large and reviewer of wines from Northern Italy, the region that’s perfected the varietal. “Each expression has its merits and uses, making this grape a key staple of any fridge.”

Originally hailing from Italy, Pinot Grigio has expanded across the globe, thriving under a variety of conditions and pushing the boundaries of what it can create.

“In Italy, we find Pinot Grigio planted across the peninsula, but in the heights of Alto Adige, Pinot Grigio gives wines of finesse, depth, and texture, all lifted by the energetic mountain acidity,” Porter says. “Conversely, wines from Delle Venezie lift like a flower garden in full bloom with vibrant citrus notes zipping across the palate. Both places do right by the grape, and neither is better than the other—just great expressions of a place and time.”

Zooming out, New World regions like California have claimed the grape as their own, crafting stellar, singular expressions.

“Don’t get me wrong; there is minerality and acidity throughout these selections,” says Tonya Pitts, a Wine Enthusiast writer at large who reviews wines from the state, but “they embody the California sunshine” 

To think—Pinot Grigio, for all its ubiquity, still impresses our reviewers. Throw in its exceptional affordability, and you’ve got a wine ready to convert the haters. Read on for our full list of outstanding Pinot Grigios for under $20.

The Best Pinot Grigios Under $20

Cooper Mountain 2023 Estate Pinot Gris (Willamette Valley)

A powerful fresh-cut grass and wet stone aroma mixes in an earthy fashion with tangy wisps of white grapefruit and lemon peel. There’s plenty of lemon power on the palate as lemon zest and lemon verbena flavors mingle in a tingly fashion with a peperoncino note. I’m matching this tart treat with a plate of crab rangoons. Best Buy. 91 Points — Michael Alberty

$16 Empire Wine

Huia 2024 Pinot Gris (Marlborough)

A spicy, waxy, herbal combo fringes a core of lemongrass and ginger on this single vineyard wine from a longstanding, high quality Marlborough estate. The palate is slippery, textural, with crystalline crunch. A standout bottling, and distinctively different to the Savvy Bs of the region. It’s food friendly, serious yet delicious, even cellar worthy. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — Christina Pickard

$18 Saratoga Wine

CK Mondavi and Family 2023 Pinot Grigio (California)

This wine is fresh, delicate, with aromas of honeydew melon, cantaloupe, white peach, and notes of orange blossom on the nose. The flavors on the palate are Moroccan lemon, white grapefruit, spice, intertwined with minerality, acidity, and a long dry finish. Pair with shrimp hot & sour soup. Best Buy. 92 Points — Tonya Pitts

$10 Total Wine & More

J Vineyards 2023 Pinot Gris (California)

This wine is zesty, crisp, with aromas of guava, cantaloupe, lime zest, and notes of white florals on the nose. The flavors on the palate are passionfruit, peach, grapefruit segments, sliding morphing into lime black pepper cream, and a dry mineral finish. Pair with smoked oysters, tuscan crackers, and sunshine. 92 Points — T.P.

$16 Total Wine & More

Raptor Ridge 2023 Vineyard Select Pinot Gris (Willamette Valley)

The wine’s preserved lemon and chamomile aromas are like sitting down with a nice cup of tea. Perhaps it is a high tea, as the wine’s lemon verbena, lavender water and lemon zest flavors collide to give the impression of a nice lemon-lavender tart. This is not your average Pinot Gris. Best Buy. 92 Points — M.A.

$16 Wine Spectrum

Pike Road 2023 Pinot Gris (Willamette Valley)

The pleasing aromatic duo of sweet lemon drop hard candies and earthy post-rain slate patio tiles is a heck of an introduction. The wine’s lemon curd and saline flavors are backlit by refreshingly crisp acidity. With a fresh texture and the bite of that acidity, this Pinot Gris will make a great pairing with fish tacos and sunshine. Best Buy. 91 Points — M.A.

$16 Esquin Wine & Spirits

Andriano 2024 Pinot Grigio (Vigneti delle Dolomiti)

From one of Alto Adige’s oldest co-ops comes this luminous Pinot Grigio. Clean-lined pear and river stone notes hover above delicate white flowers, while the palate shows the kind of precision that only high-altitude vineyards can deliver. There’s both power and grace here, like fresh snow on ancient rock. 91 Points — Jeff Porter

$20 The Cellar D’Or

Italo Cescon 2024 Pinot Grigio (Delle Venezie)

This Pinot Grigio stands out; there is a verve that is immediately present. Golden-hued with ripe, juicy pear and preserved lemon leading the charge. The palate shows impressive weight but stays light on its feet thanks to precise acidity. Finishes with a delicate dance of orange blossom and sea salt. Grilled zucchini, feta and basil salad would shine with this wine. Best Buy. 91 Points — J.P.

$15 Binny’s

The Hare & The Tortoise 2023 Pinot Gris (King Valley)

Wine from Victoria’s King Valley is a rare find Stateside. This Pinot Gris is flinty and quite shy at first sniff, the pretty orchard fruit and dried herb notes emerging as it warms in glass. There’s a lovely seamlessness to the palate, a delicacy to the fruit. Not a show pony, but a balanced and enjoyable one. 90 Points — C.P.

$18 Wine Deals

Monte Volpe 2023 Pinot Grigio (Potter Valley)

The lively, lush aromas of apple, pineapple, and nectarine are layered with notes of white flowers, acacia honey, and creamy vanilla on the nose. High acidity is mixed with citrus segments, peaches, and dried herbs with a mineral, dry finish. Pair with hard cheeses, spiced cured meats. Best Buy. 91 Points — T.P.

$18 Graziano
